view.aba.hint.intro: A simple heuristic for how much to hold in stocks: equity % = N â age. The classic N is 100; longer lifespans pushed it to 110 or 120 for more growth. view.breakeven.h1.title: // BREAK-EVEN ANALYSIS
view.breakeven.hint.intro: The most basic small-business question: how many units must you sell to cover your costs? Each unit contributes price â variable cost toward the fixed costs (its contribution margin); break-even is where that accumulated contribution exactly covers them. Add a target profit to see the volume that earns it, and your expected sales to see the margin of safety. If the contribution margin is zero or negative, no volume ever breaks even â selling more only loses more.
view.breakeven.warn.no_be: Price is at or below variable cost â every unit loses money before fixed costs. Raise price or cut variable cost.

view.brrrr.h1.title: // BRRRR
view.brrrr.hint.intro: Buy a distressed property cheap, rehab it to force appreciation, rent it, then refinance against the higher after-repair value and pull your cash back out to do it again. The deal succeeds when the cash-out refi recovers everything you put in â the cash left in the deal still earns rent, so the cash-on-cash return goes infinite.
view.brrrr.note.partial: Some cash stays in the deal; the cash-on-cash return measures what it earns.
view.brrrr.note.recovered: The refinance recovered all your cash â an infinite-return rental you can repeat.

view.burn.h1.title: // BURN RATE & RUNWAY
view.burn.hint.intro: How long the cash lasts. Gross burn is monthly expenses; net burn is expenses minus revenue â what actually drains the bank. Runway is the months of cash at that burn, simulated month by month so growing revenue extends it. If revenue overtakes expenses before the cash runs out, you reach break-even and never deplete. Updates as you type.

view.divc.h1.title: // DIVIDEND COVERAGE
view.divc.hint.intro: Is a stock's dividend sustainable? The payout ratio (dividends ÃṖ EPS) is the share of earnings paid out â over 100% means the dividend exceeds earnings, funded from cash or debt. Coverage (EPS ÃṖ DPS) is how many times earnings cover it; retention is what's plowed back. The FCF payout (dividends ÃṖ free cash flow) is a stricter test, since dividends are paid from cash, not accounting earnings. Updates as you type.

view.dr.h1.title: // DEPRECIATION RECAPTURE
view.dr.hint.intro: Depreciation lowers a rental's basis while you hold it; selling claws part of that back. For real property, the depreciation portion of the gain is unrecaptured Â§ 1250 gain, taxed at a maximum 25% rate; the appreciation above your original basis is regular long-term capital gain. Recapture is limited to the lesser of depreciation taken or the total gain â a sale at a loss recaptures nothing. NIIT (3.8%) may apply on top. Updates as you type.

view.flip.h1.title: // FIX & FLIP UNDERWRITING
view.flip.hint.intro: Two questions every flipper asks. What's the most I can pay? The 70% rule: max allowable offer = ARV Ã 70% â rehab; the 30% haircut covers holding, financing, selling, and profit. What do I make? Net profit = sale proceeds (ARV less selling costs) minus every cost â purchase, rehab, closing, the monthly carry, and the financing (points + interest). Cash-on-cash divides profit by the cash you put in (all-in cost less the loan) and annualizes by the hold. Updates as you type.

view.grm.h1.title: // GROSS RENT MULTIPLIER
view.grm.hint.intro: A fast way to compare rental prices: property price ÃṖ gross annual rent. A lower GRM is cheaper relative to the rent it produces. Scheduled rent overstates reality, so vacancy and credit loss are netted out (and other income added) into effective gross income â and an effective GRM on what's actually collected. Updates as you type.
view.guardrails.h1.title: // GUYTON-KLINGER GUARDRAILS
view.guardrails.hint.intro: Dynamic retirement withdrawals. A static 4% rule ignores how the portfolio actually does; guardrails adjust each year's draw so a bad sequence doesn't drain the account and a good one isn't under-spent. Raise with inflation â but freeze the raise in a down year; cut 10% if the withdrawal rate climbs past the upper guardrail; raise 10% if it falls below the lower one.

view.househack.h1.title: // HOUSE HACKING
view.househack.hint.intro: Buy a 2â4 unit property, live in one unit, rent the rest. The tenants' rent offsets your carrying cost (mortgage + tax + insurance + maintenance + HOA), so the money that would have been rent builds equity instead. This shows what you actually pay to live there, how it compares to renting, and the property's cash flow once you move out and rent every unit.
view.househack.note.covers: One unit's rent already covers the mortgage P&I.
view.househack.note.partial: The rented units cover part of the carrying cost â the rest is your housing budget.

view.hsatt.hint.intro: The HSA is the only account taxed favorably three ways: contributions are deductible, growth is tax-free, and withdrawals for medical expenses are tax-free. This projects an HSA against a taxable account funded with the same gross income â the taxable side invests only the after-tax amount and pays cap-gains on its growth. The gap is the dollar value of the triple-tax treatment. Updates as you type.

view.irmaa.h1.title: // MEDICARE IRMAA SURCHARGE
view.irmaa.hint.intro: High-income Medicare beneficiaries pay a surcharge on top of the standard Part B and Part D premiums. It's a cliff: cross a MAGI threshold by a single dollar and you owe the full higher tier all year â no phase-in. IRMAA is assessed per enrolled person on a two-year lookback, so 2026 premiums are set on your 2024 MAGI. That's why Roth conversions, capital gains, and RMDs taken two years before you enroll quietly raise these premiums.
view.irmaa.note: 2026 premiums (based on 2024 MAGI). Standard Part B is $202.90/mo. IRMAA is charged per enrolled individual; brackets are inflation-adjusted yearly.
view.irmaa.tip.both: MFJ only â when both spouses are enrolled, each pays the surcharge, doubling the household total

view.lke.h1.title: // Â§ 1031 LIKE-KIND EXCHANGE
view.lke.hint.intro: Swapping one investment property for another defers the gain â but only to the extent you fully reinvest. Boot (non-like-kind value you keep) is taxable now: cash received plus net debt relief (old mortgage â new mortgage, if you trade down). Recognized gain = the lesser of realized gain or total boot; the rest defers into the replacement property's carryover basis (its cost â deferred gain). A loss isn't recognized. Updates as you type.
view.lke.note.full: Fully deferred â no boot, no tax due now; the entire gain rolls into the replacement basis.
view.lmf.h1.title: // CAR LEASE PAYMENT
view.lmf.hint.intro: A lease payment is depreciation + finance fee + tax. Depreciation = (cap cost â residual) ÃṖ term â paying for the value lost while you drive. The finance fee = (cap cost + residual) Ã money factor â the interest; the money factor is a disguised rate (APR = money factor Ã 2400). Tax is on the payment in most states. Updates as you type.

view.ltvcac.h1.title: // LTV : CAC
view.ltvcac.hint.intro: Does acquiring customers pay off? LTV (lifetime value) = monthly gross profit per customer Ã average lifetime (100 ÃṖ monthly churn %) â gross profit, since you keep only the margin. CAC is the sales + marketing cost per new customer. The LTV:CAC ratio rule of thumb is 3:1; below ~1:1 you lose money on each customer, far above 3:1 you may be under-investing in growth. CAC payback is the months of gross profit to recoup the cost. Updates as you type.

view.market_gamma_regime.intro: Computes total SPY-level dealer GEX across the nearest 4 option expirations every 30 min via the existing gex_scanner + Black-Scholes gamma pipeline. Positive GEX: dealers long gamma â sell-into-rallies / buy-into-dips re-hedging â vol-suppressing, mean-reverting tape. Negative GEX: dealers short gamma â buy-rallies / sell-dips re-hedging â vol-amplifying, momentum tape. Flip moments bracket the biggest historical SPX vol expansions (Squeeze Metrics, JPM Equity Derivatives, Goldman quant desk research). Portfolio-level regime indicator â not a per-trade signal â it tells you which strategies have a tailwind vs headwind right now.

view.mm.h1.title: // MARKUP vs MARGIN
view.mm.hint.intro: Markup is profit as a percent of cost; margin is the same profit as a percent of price. They are never equal â a 50% markup is only a 33% margin, and doubling cost (100% markup, "keystone") is a 50% margin. Pricing off the wrong one erodes profit. Pick what you know â price, markup%, or margin% â and see all four. Updates as you type.
view.mm.warn.infeasible: A margin of 100% or more is impossible â price would be infinite. Lower the margin.

Below the totals table is a KILL-SWITCH that cancels every open order + closes every position across all linked brokers â destructive, gated by a typed confirmation token.

view.paycheck401k.hint.intro: How much per paycheck to hit the annual 401(k) limit by December â and whether you're about to leave free money on the table. Most plans match per paycheck, so front-loading hits the limit early and stops your contributions (and the match) for the rest of the year, unless the plan trues up the missed match. Spreading evenly captures the full match every period.

view.paycheck401k.note.trueup: You hit the limit early, but the plan's true-up pays the missed match.

view.psv.h1.title: // PENSION SURVIVOR ELECTION
view.psv.hint.intro: A pension forces a choice at retirement: single-life pays the most but stops at the retiree's death, or joint-and-survivor pays less now but continues (often reduced) to the surviving spouse. The reduction is the price of survivor protection. The pension-max alternative: take the higher single-life payment and buy life insurance for the spouse â if the payment minus the premium still beats the J&S amount, you get more income now and protect the survivor. Updates as you type.

view.rr.h1.title: // REAL (INFLATION-ADJUSTED) RETURN
view.rr.hint.intro: A nominal return overstates your gain â inflation erodes purchasing power and taxes take a cut. The Fisher equation gives the exact real return: (1 + nominal) ÃṖ (1 + inflation) â 1. The "nominal â inflation" shortcut runs a touch high. After-tax real applies the tax to the return first. Over a horizon, the real future value is the principal's purchasing power in today's dollars. Updates as you type.

view.rvs.h1.title: // RENT vs SELL
view.rvs.hint.intro: Keep the property as a rental or sell it and invest the proceeds? This compares total wealth at the end of your horizon, both assuming you liquidate at the end. Sell now: net proceeds (value â selling costs â mortgage â capital-gains tax) compounded at your alternative return. Keep: the property appreciates and throws off rental cash flow (reinvested), then sells at the horizon. Mortgage paydown is ignored (conservative for keeping). Updates as you type.

view.sira.h1.title: // SPOUSAL IRA
view.sira.hint.intro: Normally an IRA contribution needs earned income. The spousal-IRA rule lets a married couple filing jointly fund an IRA for a spouse with little or no income, as long as their combined earned income covers both contributions. Each spouse is still capped at the annual limit ($7,500 for 2026, +$1,100 catch-up at 50+). Updates as you type.

view.spia.h1.title: // SPIA INCOME
view.spia.hint.intro: A single-premium immediate annuity turns a lump sum into guaranteed income starting now. The monthly payment is the annuity that exhausts the premium over the payout period at the insurer's assumed rate â the same math as a loan payment, in reverse. The payout rate is the annual income as a percent of the premium. (Period-certain approximation; a true life annuity also prices in mortality.) Updates as you type.

view.vrp.intro: For each of the top-30 most-active symbols, computes annualised 20-day realized vol from daily closes (Yahoo cache) and ATM call implied vol from the option expiration nearest 30 DTE. SELL direction sorts by descending IV/RV â overpriced premium, candidates for short straddles / iron condors / credit spreads. BUY direction ranks ascending â underpriced premium, contrarian long-vol setups. Historical SPX mean IV/RV â 1.2-1.3; anything above ~1.6 for a single name is meaningfully rich. Refreshes every 60 min in the background.

view.yield_to_call.intro: Callable bonds: issuer can redeem at a stated price on stated dates. YTC is the IRR assuming the bond is called at the first call date. YTM assumes held to maturity. Yield-to-worst (YTW) is the lower of YTM and any YTC â that's the yield you should evaluate against, since the issuer will only call when it benefits them.
view.yield_to_call.title: YIELD CALL ÂṖ YTC vs YTM vs YTW
view.ytf.h1.title: // YEARS TO FINANCIAL INDEPENDENCE
view.ytf.hint.intro: FI is when your portfolio funds your spending indefinitely at a safe withdrawal rate â the FI number = annual expenses ÃṖ SWR (4% â 25Ã expenses). Starting from current savings and adding the income-minus-expenses gap each year (grown at your return), this finds the years to reach it. The dominant lever is the savings rate, far more than the return. Updates as you type.
